title: Padding vs margin in Tailwind
concept: Padding pushes content inward from an element's border — it's space *inside* the box. Margin pushes other elements away — it's space *outside* the box. In Tailwind, `p-4` adds 1rem of padding on all sides; `m-4` adds 1rem of margin. The asymmetric sides exist too: `px-4` is horizontal padding only, `mt-2` is margin-top, etc. The rule of thumb: if a designer asks for 'breathing room around the text inside a card,' that's padding. If they ask for 'gap between cards,' that's margin (or better — `gap-*` on a flex/grid parent).

example.code:
Card title

The p-6 gives breathing room inside. The mb-2 separates this paragraph from the heading above.
example.annotation: p-6 = padding inside the card; mb-2 = margin below the heading

alternatives.approach: Use `gap` on a flex/grid parent instead of margin on children
alternatives.whenToUse: When you control the layout container. `gap` doesn't double up like adjacent margins can, and it's easier to change in one place.
When you want consistent vertical rhythm between siblings but the parent isn't flex/grid. Slightly older pattern — modern Tailwind tends toward `gap`.

critique: Designers tend to think in 'gaps between things,' which maps to margin or gap. Developers often default to padding because it stays attached to the component. Neither is wrong — but using `gap` on a parent flex container is usually the cleanest answer when you have control over the parent.

title: Props vs state
concept: Props are what a component receives from its parent — they're read-only inputs, like function arguments. State is data the component itself owns and can change over time, declared with `useState`. The mental model: props flow down the tree; state lives inside one component. If two siblings need the same data, lift the state up to a common ancestor and pass it down as props.

example.annotation: label is a prop (passed in). count is state (owned here).

alternatives.approach: Lift state up to parent when siblings need to share it
alternatives.whenToUse: When two or more children need to read or modify the same value. The lowest common ancestor owns it.
When state transitions are complex (multiple fields, many event types). Reducers make changes more predictable.
When state is needed far apart in the tree and prop-drilling becomes painful. Don't reach for this early.

critique: The hardest part isn't knowing the rule — it's knowing where state *should* live. Beginners over-localize state and then prop-drill setters everywhere. The fix is usually to lift state up sooner than feels comfortable.

title: Server components vs client components
concept: In the Next.js App Router, every component is a Server Component by default — it runs on the server, never ships JavaScript to the browser, and can directly read files or hit databases. To use hooks like `useState`, browser APIs like `localStorage`, or any interactivity, you add `'use client'` at the top of the file. That component (and its imports) then ship to the browser. The key insight: keep `'use client'` as deep in the tree as you can. A client component can render server components passed as children, but it can't import them.

example.annotation: Server component loads data; client component handles interactivity.

alternatives.approach: Make the whole page a client component
alternatives.whenToUse: Almost never the right call in App Router. You lose streaming, lose data-fetching ergonomics, and ship more JS to the browser.

alternatives.approach: Pass server components as children of a client component
alternatives.whenToUse: When you want a client-side wrapper (e.g. layout with interactive sidebar) but the inner content is static. Children are rendered server-side and slotted in.

critique: The mental model is hardest when you're used to plain React, where everything ran in the browser. The default flipped: server is the new default. Once you stop sprinkling `'use client'` reflexively, bundles get smaller and pages get faster.

title: Why shadcn copies components into your repo
concept: shadcn/ui is not an npm package you install — it's a CLI that copies component source code directly into your `components/ui` folder. You own the code. This is the opposite of most UI libraries (Material UI, Chakra, etc.) where components are imported from a black-box dependency. The benefit: you can edit them, restyle them, and they don't go stale with the library. The cost: you're responsible for maintenance — there's no `npm update` to fix bugs.

example.code:
# Install a single component — it gets copied to components/ui/button.tsx
npx shadcn@latest add button

# Now button.tsx is YOUR file. Edit it however you want.
example.annotation: The CLI copies source, including dependencies on Radix UI primitives.

alternatives.approach: Use a traditional component library (MUI, Chakra)
alternatives.whenToUse: When you have a large team that wants strong defaults, less customization, and centralized updates. Easier to onboard juniors.

alternatives.approach: Build everything from scratch with Tailwind
alternatives.whenToUse: When you have a strong opinionated design system and don't want any opinions from a library. More work upfront.

critique: shadcn occupies a sweet spot for product designers who code: you get well-built, accessible components but full control to restyle. The 'no maintenance updates' downside matters less than it sounds — most components don't need updates often.

title: git add, commit, push — the daily three
concept: These three commands are 80% of daily git. `git add <files>` stages changes — telling git 'I want these in my next commit.' `git commit -m "message"` snapshots staged changes into the local history. `git push` uploads commits to the remote (GitHub). Think of it as: stage → snapshot → upload. The staging step exists so you can selectively include changes, but for solo work you can often `git add .` then commit.

example.code:
# See what changed
git status

# Stage all changes in the current directory
git add .

# Commit with a descriptive message
git commit -m "Add padding/margin lesson"

# Send commits to GitHub
git push
example.annotation: Run git status before and after to see what moved.

alternatives.approach: Stage specific files instead of everything
alternatives.whenToUse: When you have unrelated changes in the working tree and want to commit them separately. Cleaner history.

alternatives.approach: Use `git commit -am "..."` to stage and commit tracked files in one step
alternatives.whenToUse: When you've only modified files git is already tracking (no new files). Saves a step.

critique: The trap is forgetting `git push`. Your local commits don't go anywhere until you push. The other trap is committing too rarely — small, frequent commits make it much easier to undo a mistake.

title: What docker compose up actually does
concept: `docker compose up` reads a `docker-compose.yml` file in the current directory and starts all the services defined in it — a database, a cache, an API server, whatever. Each service runs in its own container (a lightweight isolated process with its own filesystem). The point: you get the same backend stack everyone on the team has, with one command, without installing Postgres or Redis on your machine directly.

example.code:
# docker-compose.yml
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: dev
    ports: ["5432:5432"]
  redis:
    image: redis:7
    ports: ["6379:6379"]

# Then in your terminal:
docker compose up      # foreground, logs visible
docker compose up -d   # detached (background)
docker compose down    # stop and remove containers
example.annotation: ports: "5432:5432" means container port 5432 is reachable from your laptop on 5432.

alternatives.approach: Install Postgres/Redis directly on your machine
alternatives.whenToUse: Almost never on a team. You lose version parity and 'works on my machine' bugs creep back in.

alternatives.approach: Use a hosted cloud database (Neon, Supabase, Upstash)
alternatives.whenToUse: When you need to share state with teammates or you're deploying soon anyway. Closer to production behavior.

critique: Docker has a steep first encounter, but `compose up` is the rare command that just works. If it doesn't, it's usually a port conflict (something else is on 5432) or Docker Desktop not running.

title: What a REST endpoint actually is
concept: A REST endpoint is a URL plus an HTTP method that does one specific thing on a server. `GET /api/lessons` means 'fetch the list of lessons.' `POST /api/lessons` means 'create a new lesson.' The URL identifies the resource; the method describes the action. Servers respond with a status code (200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 server error) and usually a JSON body. The frontend's job is to call these, handle responses, and update the UI.

example.annotation: Always check res.ok — fetch only rejects on network errors, not 4xx/5xx.

alternatives.whenToUse: When the frontend needs lots of nested data in one request and REST would require many round-trips. More setup, more power.
When frontend and backend are both TypeScript and share types. You get auto-completion and type safety across the wire.
When using Next.js App Router and the action is tightly coupled to a form or button. No need to define a REST endpoint at all.

critique: REST is the lingua franca — every backend can speak it, every frontend can call it. The downside is that complex UIs end up making 10+ requests per page load. Most apps live with this; some reach for GraphQL or server actions when it stings.
